caseStudy.title: Fraud Analytics for Financial Crime
caseStudy.description: Graph-based transaction monitoring across a banking network.
caseStudy.overview: A regional bank's transaction monitoring system generated thousands of false positive alerts daily, overwhelming compliance investigators and causing genuine suspicious activity to be buried in noise. The goal was to dramatically improve detection quality while reducing investigator workload.
caseStudy.problem: The rule-based monitoring system flagged 4,200 alerts per day, of which fewer than 2% required escalation. Investigators spent most of their time triaging irrelevant alerts rather than investigating genuine risk. Complex multi-entity money laundering schemes involving shell companies were consistently missed.
caseStudy.solution: A graph-based analytics layer was built on top of the transaction data, modelling relationships between accounts, entities, and transactions. Machine learning models scored alerts by combining graph features with transaction patterns, dramatically reducing the alert volume. Network visualisation tools allowed investigators to explore entity relationships interactively.

results.summary: Investigators now focus exclusively on high-quality alerts, improving the bank's ability to detect genuine financial crime while dramatically reducing compliance costs.
